title_en: Pre-Islamic Arabia and the Condition of the World Before Islam
title_ar: أحوال العرب والعالم قبيل البعثة النبوية
content_en:
Before commencing his biography of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, Ibn Kathir provides an account of the state of the world in the period immediately preceding the prophetic mission — a picture that establishes the context of spiritual, moral, and political crisis into which Islam was sent as the final divine remedy. This chapter parallels and supplements the geographical and social analysis that al-Mubarakpuri would provide in more detail in al-Rahiq al-Makhtum, but with Ibn Kathir's characteristic attention to historical and prophetic sources.

The two great empires of the age — the Sassanid Persian Empire and the Byzantine Roman Empire — were at war with each other in a prolonged conflict that had exhausted both. The Persians, under Khosrow II (Parviz), had captured Jerusalem in 614 CE, carrying away the True Cross and massacring tens of thousands of Christians. The Byzantines under Heraclius had then mounted a remarkable counteroffensive, recapturing the territories and returning the Cross to Jerusalem by 630 CE. The Quran itself references this conflict in Surah al-Rum: 'The Romans have been defeated in the nearest land, but after their defeat, they will overcome in a few years — the decision of the matter, before and after, belongs to Allah' (Quran 30:2-4). This Quranic prophecy — fulfilled within the time frame it specified — is one of the most noted predictive verses in Islamic apologetics.

Beyond these military and political realities, Ibn Kathir describes the spiritual condition of the world's great religious communities. The Jews, he notes, had departed from the pure monotheism of Musa — their religious practice had become corrupted by legalism, racial exclusivism, and the distortions that their scholars had introduced into the transmitted texts. The Christians had transformed the purely prophetic message of Isa into a theology of divine incarnation, trinitarianism, and ecclesiastical authority that bore little resemblance to the original apostolic tradition. The Zoroastrian Persians worshipped fire. The Arabs worshipped idols they had placed in the Kaaba originally built for the worship of Allah alone.

Thus the world in the late sixth century CE was, from the Quranic and prophetic perspective, in a state of spiritual corruption and moral decline across all its major civilizations. The pagan Arabs, for all their tribal violence and moral failures, retained some elements of the Abrahamic inheritance — monotheistic impulses (the Hanifs), recognition of Allah as the supreme creator, and the institution of the pilgrimage — that would make them the initial recipients of the final, comprehensive divine message. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was sent to a world that had lost its way in every direction, and the universality of that loss is part of the explanation for the universality of the mission: 'And We have not sent you except as a mercy to all the worlds' (Quran 21:107) — not to Arabia alone, not to the Arabs alone, but to every nation and civilization that had strayed from the original divine guidance.
